text: Upon entering the surgical field, the omentum is densely adhered to a necrotic gallbladder. The surgeon carefully dissects the omentum away, revealing dark brown, odorous bile leaking from the gallbladder, indicative of severe cholecystitis. A benign liver cyst is visible, and the surgeon uses ICG to delineate the liver border, ensuring precise dissection without damaging the liver capsule. The right upper quadrant shows a massive abscess, with the omentum plastered over the gallbladder and extending into the quadrant. The surgeon continues to retract the gallbladder, highlighting the extensive infection in the area.

The surgeon continues to work the omentum down using blunt dissection with a suction irrigator, aided by ICG to identify the liver edges and avoid injury. The infundibulum area shows severe necrosis with liquefied tissue, indicating gangrenous cholecystitis. The surgeon carefully dissects the thick, inflamed omentum, attempting to discern anatomical structures, with the duodenum being clearly visible.

The surgeon performs a subtotal cholecystectomy, carefully removing the surface of the gallbladder from the cystic plate while staying above the infundibulum. A large stone is identified in the infundibulum. The surgeon clips and ligates a tubular structure, likely the cystic artery, after confirming its path to the gallbladder. The gallbladder wall is further dissected from the cystic plate, and the impacted stone is flushed out using suction and irrigation to clear any remaining debris.

The surgeon inspects the gallbladder for additional stones before focusing on the right upper quadrant. A large abscess is visible, occupying the anterior surface of the liver and extending posteriorly to the right hemidiaphragm. The surgeon debrides and drains the thick, chronic abscess cavity.

The surgeon debrides the abscess cavity from the liver and right upper quadrant, using monopolar cautery to ablate the mucosa during the subtotal cholecystectomy. Specimens, including a stone, pieces of rind from the abscess cavity, and the gallbladder, are placed into an endocatch bag.

The surgeon places a hemostatic agent in the cystic plate and infundibulum to aid in hemostasis and promote scarring of the cystic duct. Two drains are positioned: one in the right upper quadrant to drain the abscess, and another in the cystic plate to monitor for bile leakage following the subtotal cholecystectomy.
